coredata.dc:title: Electrochemical process of early-stage corrosion detection based on N-doped carbon dots with superior Fe3+ responsiveness
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Zheng
Jia, Ruonan
Chen, Feng
Yan, Guilong
Tian, Weiguo
Zhang, Jinming
Zhang, Jun
coredata.dc:description: Iron corrosion is a subject of great technological importance and extensive public concern. However, the highly efficient detection of iron corrosion at early stage is still a challenging task. Herein, bright fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) with superior response to Fe3+ were prepared by simple solvothermal process based on citric acid and ammonia. The obtained CDs are able to rapidly, sensitively and selectively respond to Fe3+. The quantitative analysis showed that the CDs exhibited a linear response to Fe3+ in the range of 10 to 300 µM, with a detection limit of 0.9 μM. And the fluorescence quenching of CDs was obvious enough to be detected by the naked eyes. Such promising responsiveness of CDs offers a great opportunity for real-time and visual detection of Fe3+ during electrochemical corrosion process. In addition, due to the excellent stability and solubility of CDs, patterned papers and hydrogels have been fabricated utilizing cellulose and PVA as matrices. The as-prepared biocompatible, environmental-friendly and disposable CDs based fluorescent materials were successfully used for detecting the degree of iron corrosion. This could provide a simple and visual strategy for monitoring the safety of structural metal materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Iron corrosion
Carbon dots
Fe3+ detection
Sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescent detection towards Fe3+ and other metal ions

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Youth Innovation Promotion Association of the Chinese Academy of Sciences

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by Research start-up funds of Chengdu University of Technology and the Youth Innovation Promotion Association CAS ( 2018040 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Synthetic route of CDs and photographs of CDs aqueous solution before and after adding Fe3+ under visible light and UV light; (b) TEM image of the CDs, Inset: particles size distribution measured by TEM; (c) XRD pattern of the CDs; (d) FTIR spectrum of the CDs.
(a) XPS spectrum of the CDs and high-resolution deconvoluted profiles of (b) C 1 s, (c) N 1 s, (d) O 1 s of the CDs.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of CDs with different concentrations of Fe3+ (0–1000 μM). (b) Plot of the ratio of fluorescence intensity change at 450 nm ((I0 − I)/I0) versus the concentration of Fe3+. I0 and I represent the fluorescence intensity at 450 nm before and after the addition of Fe3+. Inset: images of the CDs aqueous solutions treated with a series of different concentrations of Fe3+ under visible light and UV light. (c) Linear fitting of the ratio of fluorescence intensity change at 450 nm ((I0-I)/I0) versus the concentration of Fe3+ (10–300 μM). (d) The ratio of the fluorescence intensity of CDs at 450 nm (I/I0) after and before the addition of Fe3+, Fe2+ and other metal ions.
Images of CDs-based test papers and hydrogels under sunlight and UV light before and after addition of Fe3+.
Schematic illustration of the corrosion detection mechanism based on CDs.

(a) The fluorescence spectra of CDs upon addition of corrosion solutions after the iron plates have been immersed in corrosion medium (0.1 M NaCl) for different time. (b) The fluorescence intensity change ratio at 450 nm ((I0 − I)/I0) versus immersion time of iron in 0.1 M NaCl. (c) The fluorescence spectra of CDs upon addition of iron corrosion solution using 0.1 M and 1 M NaCl solutions as eroding solution after 12 h immersion. (d) The images of CDs based fluorescent materials before and after adding corrosion solution under UV light. Ex: 365 nm.

Weight loss ratio (W0-W)/W0 versus immersion time of iron in (a) 0.1 M NaCl solution and (b) 1 M NaCl solution. Inset: corrosion rates when the iron plates have been immersed in NaCl solutions for 12 h.
. (a) 2D and (b) 3D AFM images of the iron plate immersed in 1 M NaCl solution for 12 h, and (c) height profile made along the marked line in (a).
Comparison of this prepared CDs with previous methods and materials for Fe3+ detection.

row.entry: Electrochemical oxidation of graphite electrode CQDs

head.ce:title: Electrochemical process of early-stage corrosion detection based on N-doped carbon dots with superior Fe3+ responsiveness

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials and Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Chengdu University of Technology, Chengdu 610059, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials and Chemistry & Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Materials and Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Chengdu University of Technology, Chengdu 610059, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100190, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100190, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Blue-emitting carbon dots exhibited visual, rapid, selective and accurate detection of Fe3+ during electrochemical corrosion process according to the fluorescence (FL) intensity quenching, which can be used for the early-stage corrosion detection.

Iron corrosion is a subject of great technological importance and extensive public concern. However, the highly efficient detection of iron corrosion at early stage is still a challenging task. Herein, bright fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) with superior response to Fe3+ were prepared by simple solvothermal process based on citric acid and ammonia. The obtained CDs are able to rapidly, sensitively and selectively respond to Fe3+. The quantitative analysis showed that the CDs exhibited a linear response to Fe3+ in the range of 10 to 300 µM, with a detection limit of 0.9 μM. And the fluorescence quenching of CDs was obvious enough to be detected by the naked eyes. Such promising responsiveness of CDs offers a great opportunity for real-time and visual detection of Fe3+ during electrochemical corrosion process. In addition, due to the excellent stability and solubility of CDs, patterned papers and hydrogels have been fabricated utilizing cellulose and PVA as matrices. The as-prepared biocompatible, environmental-friendly and disposable CDs based fluorescent materials were successfully used for detecting the degree of iron corrosion. This could provide a simple and visual strategy for monitoring the safety of structural metal materials.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to its excellent load-bearing capacity and high ductility, iron and its alloys have been extensively used in the manufacture industry [1]. However, most of iron products suffer from corrosion damage in the presence of humid atmosphere, salt solution, acid, and alkali. If it is left unnoticed, iron corrosion will dramatically reduce the mechanical properties of iron components, leading to huge economic losses and great threat to life safety. To ensure effective inhibition of corrosion, the detection of corrosion degree is therefore of great significance. And corrosion has to be recognized at early-stage, in order to intervene at the point where it is necessary to prevent consequential damages. Thus, the detection of early corrosion is indispensable to avoid critical engineering failures. As results, a detection system that can detect corrosion at its infancy has great research value. There are several methods to detect corrosion, such as weight loss measurement, electrochemical methods, electrical resistance probes, and linear polarization resistance (LPR) [2,3]. However, these methods involve the use of sophisticated instruments and time-consuming operation, which are unable to accurately and rapidly reveal the degree of corrosion in situ. For this reason, a real-time and visual detection method for corrosion is urgently needed. In the electrochemical process, dissociation of metal occurs on the solid-liquid interface during corrosion. Thus, the concentration of Fe3+/Fe2+ in the solution can be used as indicator to reflect the corrosion degree of iron components. In recent years, optical sensors have attracted more and more attention due to their excellent selectivity, remarkable sensitivity, and easy operation [4]. To effectively detect early-stage corrosion, an ideal optical sensor sensitive to Fe3+/Fe2+ is desirable, which should ideally exhibit effective sensitivity, unprecedented resolution, accuracy, simple preparation, short analysis time as well as easy visualization.

Carbon dots (CDs) have attracted the attention of many researchers [5–8]. Compared with conventional semiconductor quantum dots containing heavy metals [9], CDs exhibit some excellent characteristics, such as stable chemical properties, low toxicity and tunable photoluminescence [10]. Because of the good biocompatibility and environmental friendliness, CDs are considered to be promising alternatives to semiconductor quantum dots. Taking advantage of the prominent photoluminescence properties, the CDs have been widely used for ion sensing, bioimaging, and photocatalysis [11–15]. Recently, the N-doped graphene quantum dots for effective and label-free detection of Fe3+ have been developed and successfully applied to monitor Fe3+ in water samples [16]. And fluorescent sensors based on S-doped carbon dots for Fe3+ detection have been fabricated by a simple and straightforward hydrothermal method. The detection limit of the S-doped carbon dots was 0.1 µM [17]. Thus, CDs can be readily applied as optical sensors for real-time and visual detection of early-stage corrosion.

In this work, we employed citric acid as the carbon precursor and ammonia as the nitrogen dopant to synthesize fluorescent CDs by a facile solvothermal treatment. The synthesized CDs with bright blue fluorescence can selectively, visually, and effectively detect Fe3+ over other metal ions. The CDs-based pattern papers and hydrogels have been fabricated with cellulose and PVA as matrices. Due to the fluorescence quenching of CDs, the prepared disposable CDs materials that are successfully demonstrated for the detection of iron corrosion through the dual test modes of instrument and naked-eyes, provide an easy and visual method to monitor the safety of metal materials.

All chemicals were commercially available and used as received without additional purification. Dimethylformamide (DMF, AR), anhydrous citric acid (CA, AR), ammonia (25%, AR) and urea (99%, AR) were purchased from Aladdin Chemicals Ltd (Shanghai). AR-grade metals salts NaCl, CaCl2, Al(NO3)3, Mg(NO3)2, Zn(NO3)2, ZnCl2, CdCl2, FeSO4, Sr(NO3)2, NaH2PO2, Na2CO3, Fe(NO3)3 were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Microcrystalline cellulose was purchased from J& K Scientific Ltd. 1-Allyl-3-methylimidazolium chloride (AmimCl) ionic liquid was synthesized in our laboratory. PVA (2488) was purchased from Shanghai Chenqi Chemical Technology Co., Ltd.

Anhydrous citric acid (1.0 g) was added into DMF (15 mL). The solution was stirred until a homogeneous solution was formed. Then, ammonia solution (5 mL) was added drop by drop while stirring. After that the solution was transferred to a poly (tetrafluoroethylene) -lined autoclave (50 mL). After heating at 200 °C for 10 h, dark brown suspension was obtained. Then, the suspension was centrifuged at high speed (10,000 rad/min) for 20 min to remove large or agglomerated particles. Unless stated otherwise, the CDs solution was diluted with deionized water for use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent detection towards Fe3+ and other metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: 50 μL stock solution of CDs was diluted 100 times with deionized water. And the commercial metal salts were dissolved in deionized water. Fe(NO3)3 was dissolved and diluted with different quantities of deionized water to obtain a series of Fe3+ solutions with known concentration. Upon adding metal ions solution, the fluorescence spectra of CDs at 365 nm excitation wavelength were measured to study the fluorescence response of CDs towards Fe3+ and other metal ions.
The CDs aqueous solutions were brushed onto the filter papers. After drying, CDs-based test papers were obtained.

Microcrystalline cellulose (0.25 g) was added into 1-allyl-3-methylimidazolium chloride (AmimCl, 10 g). The mixture was stirred vigorously at 80 °C to get a homogeneous solution. Then, the solution was slowly poured into 12-well plate, and kept in a vacuum oven for 2 h to remove air bubbles. Subsequently, the plate was immersed in the deionized water as coagulation bath for 48 h. The deionized water was changed every other day to completely remove AmimCl. Finally, the obtained cellulose hydrogels were immersed in CDs aqueous solutions for 1 day to get the cellulose/CDs composite hydrogels.

PVA (1 g) was added into deionized water of 10 mL. Then the mixture was stirred vigorously at 80 °C to get a homogeneous solution. The PVA solution was slowly mixed with CDs stock solution. The PVA/CDs solution was kept in an ultrasonic cleaning machine for 15 min to remove air bubbles.

The iron plates were cut into pieces as specimens. Before weight loss detection, iron plates were carefully washed by ionized water and dried in an oven. Then, the iron specimens were immersed into different concentration of NaCl solutions for a certain time. After that, the specimens were removed, washed and dried. Then mean corrosion rate (ν, mg/m2·h) are calculated as follows [2]:
ν =
W 0
- W
St
where W0 and W represent the weight of iron specimens before and after immersion in NaCl solution. S is the surface of the specimen dipped in the corrosive solution and t is the corrosion time.

The fluorescence intensity of CDs at 450 nm was recorded three times before adding Fe3+ to calculate the standard deviation of the control group. Then, according to the linear fitting plot of the fluorescence intensity change ratio of CDs at 450 nm ((I0 − I)/I0) versus the concentration of Fe3+, the detection limit (LOD) of the synthesized CDs towards Fe3+ was calculated using the following formula [1]:
L O D =
3 S
b
where S is the standard deviation of CDs before adding Fe3+ and b is the slope of the linear line.

Fluorescence emission and excitation spectra were measured on HITACHI F-320 fluorescence spectrometer. The morphology of the CDs was investigated by TEM (JEM-2100F). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was recorded by AXIS ULTRA DLD from Kratos Analytical Ltd. The FTIR spectra were measured by VERTEX 70 from Bruker Corporation. X-ray diffraction (XRD) was conducted by RigakuSmart Lab 9Kw from Rigaku Corporation. The AFM images of the corroded iron plate was carried out by BRUKER Dimension Icon.

For effective utilization in optoelectronic devices, N is considered as a promising dopant of nanomaterials [18,19]. Herein, CDs were synthesized by a facile solvothermal treatment, using citric acid as the carbon precursor and ammonia as the nitrogen dopant (Fig. 1 a). In this process, citric acid and ammonia may undergo four stages in the formation of CDs: condensation, polymerization, carbonization and passivation, as illustrated in Fig. S1. The synthesized CDs exhibited bright blue fluorescence, which could be recognized under a UV lamp. And it is easy to observe that the blue fluorescence of CDs quenched instantly after the addition of Fe3+ solution. Under visible light, the light-yellow color of the CDs solution became darker at the same time. The morphology of the synthesized CDs was identified by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As shown in Fig. 1b, CDs have spherical shapes and they were highly dispersed in the aqueous solution without aggregation. The images inserted in Fig. 1b demonstrated that the particle size distribution of CDs was 2.5–7 nm and the average nanoparticle size was approximately 4.6 nm. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of CDs is seen in Fig. 1c. The XRD pattern showed a broad peak at 20° and a sharp peak at 26.7°, which may be attributed to the amorphous and graphitic structures of CDs as reported [20]. The broad peak at 20° was caused by the increase of the interlayer distance due to the steric hindrance of the functional groups on the graphene. The peak at 26.7° indicated the interlayer stacking in CDs, which is similar to that in graphite. To further reveal the intrinsic structure of the prepared CDs, Raman spectrum was conducted from 600 cm−1 to 2400 cm−1 as shown in Fig. S2. Two obvious peaks were observed. The D band at 1372 cm−1 described the disordered structure of the CDs and the G band at 1574 cm−1 referred to the graphitic sp2 hybridization. The result is consistent with the XRD pattern, suggesting that close packing of highly conjugated sp2 domains is part of the core of the CDs. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra analysis was carried out to investigate the chemical structure of the CDs. In Fig. 1d, the NH groups exhibited a peak at 1560 cm−1. Other peaks at 1710 cm−1 and 1407 cm−1 were observed, corresponding to the CO stretching vibration and CN vibration, respectively. The FTIR results indicated the presence of amino (NH2), carboxyl (COOH), and hydroxyl (OH) groups on the surface of the CDs [21,22].

The chemical structure of the synthesized CDs was further confirmed by XPS analysis. Fig. 2 a showed the XPS spectrum of CDs, and it can be seen that there are three peaks at 285.14, 399.78, and 531.88 eV, corresponding to the peaks of C, N, and O, respectively. The XPS spectrum revealed that the proportions of C, N, and O were 46.47%, 42.25%, and 11.28%, respectively. The high-resolution C 1 s spectrum of the CDs can be fitted into three peaks in Fig. 2b. The peak at 284.8 eV was attributed to the CC bond, and the peak at 285.73 eV can be attributed to CN, while the peaks at 288.27 eV could be ascribed to CO/CN [23,24]. For N 1 s, as seen in Fig. 2c, the XPS spectrum can be fitted into pyridinic N (399.41 eV) and pyrollic N (400.05 eV), respectively [25]. The deconvoluted spectra of O 1 s in Fig. 2d displayed three peaks at 531.41, 532.1, and 533.08 eV, which could be ascribed to COH, CO, and CO/CN [26,27]. The results of the XPS analysis agreed with the FTIR analysis. The functional groups on the surface of CDs not only contribute to the efficient luminescence but also help to stabilize CDs in the aqueous environment. In order to ensure that the CDs can be applied in corrosion-sensitive environment, we verified the solubility and stability of the CDs. As shown in Fig. S3, the CDs were dispersed in water and NaCl solutions for 5 days. It is obvious that no precipitate was formed and the fluorescence intensity of the CDs at 450 nm remained almost unchanged, suggesting the practical application ability of CDs in corrosive solution.

The fluorescence spectra of the prepared blue-emitting CDs were shown in Fig. 3 a, which exhibited a characteristic emission band at 450 nm. Upon addition of Fe3+, the fluorescence intensity at 450 nm quenched with the increase of Fe3+ concentration. The plot of the fluorescence quenching ratio of CDs versus the concentration of Fe3+ was demonstrated in Fig. 3b. It was found that the prepared CDs can readily respond to Fe3+ in a wide range of 10–1 × 103 μM. And the fluorescence quenching ratio increased rapidly in the concentration range of 10–400 μM, suggesting the excellent response sensitivity of CDs. The inset images in Fig. 3b displayed the color and fluorescence changes of CDs aqueous solution under visible and UV light upon addition of a series of Fe3+ solution. It can be readily observed that the color became darker and the fluorescence of CDs gradually disappeared along with the increase of the concentration of Fe3+, which was obvious enough to be recognized by the naked eyes. So, we can visually determine the concentration of Fe3+ according to the different colors and fluorescence. This strategy is rapid, convenient and easy-to-operate. Furthermore, it turned out that the fluorescence quenching ratio was linearly proportional to the concentration of Fe3+ in the range of 10–300 μM as seen in Fig. 3c. Based on the calibration equation, the Fe3+ concentration can be quantitatively detected [28]. And the limit of detection (LOD) of CDs for Fe3+ was calculated to be 0.9 μM [1]. The fluorescence quenching of CDs may originate from the interaction between CDs and Fe3+ ions. On the basis of the XPS and FTIR analysis, the surface of the prepared CDs tends to be composed of functional groups, such as OH, COOH, NH2 and CO. The active organic groups can readily interact with Fe3+, leading to the formation of complexes of Fe3+-CDs. Finally, electron-transfer occurred which led to the fluorescence quenching of CDs [29]. The sensing performance of previous methods and materials towards Fe3+ has been summarized in Table 1 . It can be seen that compared with these methods and materials, the prepared CDs exhibit equivalent or superior responsiveness towards Fe3+. Thus, through the dual-mode detection of Fe3+ by naked eyes and fluorescence spectrometer, qualitative and quantitative detection of Fe3+ can be achieved, which provides a great opportunity for the real-time and visual monitoring of iron corrosion.

The durability of metal-based constructions is mainly limited by corrosion, especially in the presence of humid atmosphere, salt solutions, acids and alkalis. To eliminate the interference of the corrosion-sensitive environment, the selective response of CDs towards Fe3+ is of significant importance [43]. Therefore, the response of CDs towards other metal ions was investigated. In Fig. 3d, it can be seen that other metal ions including Fe2+ had little influence on the emission intensity of CDs except Fe3+. We also tested whether or not GSH, urea, Vc and anions could be detected by CDs (seen Fig. S4). Obviously, the fluorescence of CDs towards GSH, urea, Cl-, NO3 −, SO4 2-, H2PO2 2- remained almost unchanged, while Vc and CO3 2– led to fluorescence quenching of CDs to be 20% and 40%, respectively. However, upon addition of Fe3+, the fluorescence intensity of CDs was reduced by more than 90%. That is to say, the Fe3+ detection with CDs is unable to be interfered by the environments which makes it possible to detect corrosion degree of iron in practice. The possible mechanism of selective response to Fe3+ is due to the fact that Fe3+ bound to the hydroxyl group present on the surface of CDs, resulting in the formation of Fe3+ complex chelate [44]. After that, the electrons were transferred from the excited state to the half-filled 3d orbital of Fe3+, which caused the recombination of non-radiative electron-hole pairs [45].

By simple coating and blending processes, CDs based test papers and composite hydrogels were fabricated (Fig. 4 ). The CDs-based test papers and composite hydrogels are considered to be biocompatible and biodegradable due to the characteristics of PVA and cellulose. As displayed in Fig. 4, CDs-based test papers and hydrogels gave an obvious fluorescence-off upon addition of Fe3+. Compared with CDs aqueous solutions, CDs-based test papers and hydrogels are easier to use and operate, thus they have a broader application prospect. For example, CDs-based smart tag can be readily applied to detect the corrosion degree of iron objects under real conditions without destruction by naked-eyes, which is simple and effective [46].

The electrochemical corrosion process of iron was illustrated in Fig. S5. According to the reaction equation, dissociation occurs on the solid–liquid surface. Fe2+ is gradually released to the surrounding environment. In the presence of O2, Fe2+ will be oxidized into Fe3+ and Fe(OH)3 forms. In the suspension of Fe(OH)3, Fe3+ will be released from the dissociation process until the equilibrium state is reached. Thus, Fe3+ can be recognized as an indicator to reflect the corrosion degree of iron. The detection mechanism was graphically illustrated in Fig. 5 . In NaCl solution, iron tends to corrode, and Fe3+ will be gradually released into the solution. The concentration of Fe3+ increases with the increase of corrosion degree. When the CDs are added, the fluorescence of CDs will be quenched by the Fe3+ ions. Based on the fluorescence intensity changes, iron corrosion degree can be determined as a result. Also, due to the superior responsiveness of CDs towards Fe3+, the fluorescence “turn-off” phenomenon can be obviously detected by the naked-eyes and fluorescence spectrometer.

We used iron plate as an example. Firstly, the iron plates were soaked in 0.1 M NaCl solutions for corrosion diagnosis. With prolonged corrosion time, the corrosion solutions were sampled at regular intervals. Then, the corrosion solution with the same quality was added to the CDs solution for fluorescence test. As displayed in Fig. 6 a, the fluorescence intensity of CDs at 450 nm reduced along with the prolonged corrosion time. Utilizing the NaCl solution as control, the fluorescence of CDs was unchanged. It is noticeable that the fluorescence of CDs quenched obviously when the iron plate was soaked in NaCl solution for 30 min, and the iron plate was at the early corrosion state. It indicates the availability of CDs for early-stage corrosion detection. In Fig. 6b, the fluorescence intensity change ratio (I0-I)/I0 versus immersion time of iron was investigated. The results showed that longer immersion time of the iron plates led to more quenching of the fluorescence intensity. That is to say that the fluorescence “turn-off” of CDs readily responds to the corrosion degree of iron. After 8 h, the (I0-I)/I0 decreased slightly due to the interference of the dissociation process of Fe(OH)3. We also tested the response of CDs to iron corrosion in different corrosive media. It can be seen in Fig. 6c that the iron plates in 0.1 M NaCl solution for 12 h led to more intense fluorescence quenching than that in 1 M NaCl. This showed that the iron plates were eroded more seriously in 0.1 M NaCl than in 1 M NaCl. Also, the CDs were mixed with PVA to obtain the fluorescent smart tag for corrosion diagnosis. In Fig. 6d, the CDs/PVA material was fabricated into pattern on the surface of aluminum foil. Upon addition of eroding solution on the center of the pattern, the fluorescence quenched obviously. It revealed that the response of the smart tag towards corrosion is excellent to be easily detected by the naked-eyes.

To verify the corrosion process of iron, the weight loss ratios of iron were calculated. As illustrated in Fig. 7 a and b, it is logical that the weight loss ratios increased as the immersion time prolonged, because the iron was gradually dissociated into the corrosive medium in the process. That is the reason to induce the fluorescence quenching of CDs. It can be seen that the weight loss ratios of iron plates in 0.1 M and 1 M NaCl solutions for 12 h were 0.15% and 0.10%, and the corrosion rates of iron plates in 0.1 M and 1 M NaCl solutions for 12 h were calculated to be 376.5 mg/(m2·h) and 253.1 mg/(m2·h), respectively. Thus, 0.1 M NaCl solution will readily cause more obvious fluorescence quenching of CDs than 1 M NaCl solution. The weight loss experiments of iron are consistent with the fluorescence spectra results in Fig. 6c. Besides, AFM tests were employed to further demonstrate the corrosion fact during fluorescence test. As shown in Fig. 8 , the iron plates in 1 M NaCl solution for 12 h exhibited large and deep pits on the surface (Fig. 8a and b). This is also evidenced by the height profile of the iron surface made along the marked line. The profile of corroded surface showed large fluctuation of about 80 nm (Fig. 8c). As control, the surface morphology of the iron plate before corrosion has been tested (seen Fig. S6). It is obvious that the iron surface was flat and smooth with small pits. The fluctuation along the marked line was about 6 nm. The results indicates that remarkable corrosion occurred in the NaCl solution. And with the quenching of the fluorescence of the CDs, the corrosion of the iron plate indeed happened.

Simple, fast, accurate and visual detection of corrosion degree at early-stage without destruction has great significance in ensuring the safety of materials. Herein, blue-emitting CDs were developed via a simple solvothermal method with citric acid and ammonia as the carbon source and the nitrogen dopant. The CDs can rapidly, sensitively and selectively respond to Fe3+. The quantitative analysis showed that the fluorescence of CDs linearly responded to Fe3+ in the range of 10–300 µM. The LOD of the CDs towards Fe3+ was 0.9 µM. And the CDs exhibited no response to other metal ions and common anions. Taking iron plates as example, the prepared CDs were successfully employed as fluorescent sensor for real-time and visual detection of early corrosion. Also, disposable and environmental-friendly CDs-based test papers and hydrogels were fabricated, which are expected to be used practically as smart tag with high sensitivity and selectivity for metal corrosion detection.
All relevant data are included in this Article and its Supplementary Information files.

Zheng Liu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Ruonan Jia: Validation, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Investigation, Funding acquisition. Feng Chen: Methodology, Formal analysis. Guilong Yan: Methodology, Formal analysis. Weiguo Tian: Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Jinming Zhang: Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Jun Zhang: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by Research start-up funds of Chengdu University of Technology and the Youth Innovation Promotion Association CAS (2018040).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2021.08.058.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
